subject: Physics
solution: Given $R_2=2 R_1$ $\begin{aligned} & M_1=\sigma \times \pi R_1^2=M_o \\ & M_2=\sigma \times \pi R_2^2=M_o \\ & M_2=\sigma \times \pi R_2^2=\sigma \times \pi\left[2 R_1\right]^2=\sigma \times 4 \pi R_1^2=4 M_o \end{aligned}$ $\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{\frac{M_1 R_1^2}{2}}{\frac{M_2 R_2^2}{2}}=\frac{M_1 R_1^2}{M_2 R_2^2}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{16}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Each cylinder has mass $M/4$, radius $R$, length $L$.
Axis through midpoints of top and bottom sides (along y-direction).
Top/bottom cylinders (perpendicular to axis, axis through midpoint):
$I = \frac{M_c R^2}{4} + \frac{M_c L^2}{12}$ each.
Left/right cylinders (parallel to axis, distance $L/2$ from it):
$I = \frac{M_c R^2}{2} + M_c(L/2)^2$ each.
Total $= 2\left(\frac{M_c R^2}{4}+\frac{M_c L^2}{12}\right) + 2\left(\frac{M_c R^2}{2}+\frac{M_c L^2}{4}\right)$
$= \frac{M}{2}\left(\frac{3R^2}{4}+\frac{L^2}{3}\right) = \frac{3MR^2}{8}+\frac{ML^2}{6}$.

subject: Physics
solution:
The rod is pivoted at a distance $\frac{L}{3}$ from one end. The centre of mass is at a distance $\frac{L}{6}$ from the pivot.
Using energy conservation:
$mg\frac{\ell}{6} = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$
Moment of inertia about the pivot (using parallel axis theorem):
$I = \frac{m\ell^2}{12} + m\left(\frac{\ell}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{m\ell^2}{12} + \frac{m\ell^2}{36} = \frac{m\ell^2}{9}$
$mg\frac{\ell}{6} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{m\ell^2}{9} \cdot \omega^2$
$\omega^2 = \frac{9g}{6 \cdot \frac{\ell}{2}} = \frac{3g}{\ell}$
$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{3g}{\ell}}$

Therefore, required energy loss ΔE=250 J

subject: Physics
solution:
Using the equation of rotational kinematics $\theta = \omega_0 t + \dfrac{1}{2} \alpha t^2$.

Since the wheel is initially at rest, $\omega_0 = 0$.

Angle rotated in the first $2\text{ s}$:

$\theta_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} \alpha (2)^2 = 2\alpha$

Angle rotated in the first $4\text{ s}$:

$\theta_{total} = \dfrac{1}{2} \alpha (4)^2 = 8\alpha$

Angle rotated in the next $2\text{ s}$:

$\theta_2 = \theta_{total} - \theta_1 = 8\alpha - 2\alpha = 6\alpha$

The ratio is:

$\dfrac{\theta_2}{\theta_1} = \dfrac{6\alpha}{2\alpha} = 3$

Answer: $3$

Substituting values: $0.05 \times 9.8 - 0.75 \times 0.02 = I \times 50$ gives $0.49 - 0.015 = 50I$.
Therefore $I = 9.5 \times 10^{-3}$ kg·m²

subject: Physics
solution:
The transformation $\vec{r} \to -\vec{r}$ is known as a parity transformation (inversion through the origin).
Vectors that change sign under parity are called polar vectors, while those that do not change sign are called axial vectors (or pseudovectors).
Velocity is defined as $\vec{v} = \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt}$. Since $\vec{r} \to -\vec{r}$, we have $\vec{v} \to -\vec{v}$.
Acceleration is defined as $\vec{a} = \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt}$. Since $\vec{v} \to -\vec{v}$, we have $\vec{a} \to -\vec{a}$.
Linear momentum is defined as $\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$. Since $\vec{v} \to -\vec{v}$, we have $\vec{p} \to -\vec{p}$.
Angular momentum is defined as $\vec{L} = \vec{r} \times \vec{p}$.
Under the transformation $\vec{r} \to -\vec{r}$ and $\vec{p} \to -\vec{p}$, the cross product becomes:
$\vec{L}' = (-\vec{r}) \times (-\vec{p}) = \vec{r} \times \vec{p} = \vec{L}$.
Thus, angular momentum does not flip its sign under the transformation $\vec{r} \to -\vec{r}$.

subject: Physics
solution:
Given, volume of block $=\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)^3=10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^3$
Let density of block $=\rho \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$
mass of block $=\rho \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}$
Buoyant Force $\left(\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}}\right)=1000 \times \frac{10^{-3}}{2} \times 10=5 \mathrm{~N}$
F.B.D. of blocks
Balancing torque about point O , we get
$\begin{aligned}
& \operatorname{mg}\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)-\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}}\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)=0.2 \mathrm{~g}\left(25 \times 10^{-2}\right) \\ & \rho \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 2-10=50 \\ & \rho=3000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3
\end{aligned}$
Hence, mass of block $=\rho \times 10^{-3}$
$=3000 \times 10^{-3}=3 \mathrm{~kg}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Moment of inertia of the rod about axis $AB$ passing through one of its ends:
$I_{\text{rod}} = \dfrac{ML^2}{3} = \dfrac{40 \times (3)^2}{3} = \dfrac{40 \times 9}{3} = 120\text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$

Moment of inertia of the solid sphere about the same axis $AB$, using the parallel axis theorem $I = I_{\text{cm}} + md^2$:
Mass $m = 10\text{ kg}$, radius $R$, distance from the centre of the sphere to axis $AB$ is $d = 3\text{ m}$.

$I_{\text{sphere}} = \dfrac{2}{5}mR^2 + md^2 = \dfrac{2}{5}(10)R^2 + 10(3)^2 = 4R^2 + 90$

Equating the two moments of inertia:
$120 = 4R^2 + 90$
$4R^2 = 30$
$R^2 = \dfrac{15}{2}$
$R = \sqrt{\dfrac{15}{2}}$

Comparing with $R = \sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha}{2}}$:
$\alpha = 15$

Hence, $\alpha = 15$.
